nama: Yéné waitogh siam, kimbam yufaroteng Figin si, ndauam yufaroteng téfene si a ndau yuram yénmufaroteng yénd, orayégh téfnaram téfene si.
english: In this story, the pig represents Tok Pisin, the garden represents our language and the owner of the garden represents us, the speakers of our language.
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: Yéndfé kaka orayégh ár téfene sifaf, so fété tumen norayam téfene si.
english: We as native speakers of our language, should always be speaking our language.
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: Yéna efogh em ánde yénd so norayam Fisin si, wufogh si, njam yénd norayam yémafé efe fénaté amé kanké téfnar em a yau mat em téfene si.
english: There are times when we need to speak Tok Pisin, for example when we are speaking with people who have a different mother tongue and don’t know our language.
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: Yénd njam Fisin siro norayam téfenjo kénjun, yénd amé kanké si téfnar, téfene ambum Fisin siro so náyáreta a so káyátongi Fisin si téfnarro,
english: If we make it a habit of speaking Tok Pisin amongst ourselves, the native speakers of our mother tongue, our children will then only hear Tok Pisin and so end up only speaking Tok Pisin.
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: Yéné ende yéndfem téfenjo si yékmetam kérot. Ndené
english: In this way we are putting our own language to death.
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: fété kimbam ndermae ndau yasérmbotam, yénémamén nde Fisin siam so féyotaro yasérmbote téfene si ambino.
english: Just as the pig destroyed the garden, so Tok Pisin could completely destroy our language.
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: Féyo ndermae so téfene si yárawenam wasérmboghta?
english: So how can we protect our language from being destroyed?
text_title: A Story about a Pig  and a Garden

nama: Fém njam afá-amé ném mé so noraye téfene si fene ambumafefaf a ákiafefaf, yau Tok Fisin. Fém nja
english: If you are a parent you must continue to speak our language to your children and grandchildren, not Tok Pisin.
